topic: spring in Stockholm — quantifying mood improvement with daylight

turns.content: Stockholm is getting approximately 16 hours of daylight now and I can feel the difference in a way that is almost chemical. I have been tracking my mood on a daily 1 to 10 scale since November and the correlation with daylight hours is honestly striking.
That's a great dataset to have — five months of daily mood tracking. What does the correlation look like? And is it a gradual climb or did you notice a specific inflection point?

The correlation coefficient between daily mood score and daylight hours is 0.71, which is stronger than I expected. The inflection point was around the spring equinox in late March. From November through February my average was 5.2 out of 10. March was 5.9. April so far is 6.8. That is a 31 percent improvement from the winter baseline, which is not trivial.

A 0.71 correlation is remarkably strong for something as noisy as daily mood data. The jump from 5.2 to 6.8 is significant. Are there confounding variables you've been tracking alongside daylight? Things like exercise frequency, sleep quality, work stress?

I track sleep hours and exercise sessions as well. Sleep has been relatively stable — averaging 7.1 hours in winter and 7.3 now, so a marginal increase. Exercise is up slightly because the weather permits more outdoor activity. But when I control for exercise frequency, the daylight-mood correlation only drops to 0.64, which is still substantial. I am fairly confident daylight is doing most of the work.

That's a well-controlled analysis. The fact that the correlation holds after controlling for exercise suggests the effect is largely photobiological — direct impact of light exposure on serotonin, circadian rhythm regulation, that kind of thing. Have you done anything specific to maximize your light exposure, like morning walks or a daylight lamp during winter?

I used a 10,000 lux lamp from November through February, 30 minutes each morning. Without it I think the winter numbers would have been lower. Last year I did not use one and my recollection, though not systematically tracked, is that I was more consistently at a 4 to 4.5 range. So the lamp may have added approximately one full point to my winter baseline. That is a meaningful intervention.

A full point improvement from the lamp alone is a strong result. That's the difference between 'consistently below average' and 'manageable.' It sounds like you've essentially built yourself a personal SAD management protocol through tracking. Do you plan to continue the daily mood logging through summer, or do you consider the interesting part of the dataset complete?

I will continue through the full annual cycle. The summer data is actually important because Stockholm gets nearly 19 hours of daylight in June. If the mood score plateaus around 7 or 8 rather than continuing to climb linearly, that would suggest a ceiling effect, which is what I hypothesize. There is probably a saturation point beyond which more daylight does not produce more mood benefit. I would like to find that threshold.

That's a fascinating hypothesis — and it makes physiological sense. There's likely a point of diminishing returns where your serotonin system is fully activated and additional light doesn't add much. Finding that threshold would also help you plan for next winter — if you know the mood-maximizing daylight level, you could try to simulate it with the lamp more precisely. Do you expect the decline in autumn to be symmetrical to the spring improvement?

That is one of the questions I want to answer. My hypothesis is that the decline will lag behind the daylight reduction — a kind of mood inertia from the summer months. Psychologically, I think the memory of feeling good provides a buffer for a few weeks into autumn. But by late October the buffer depletes. I will know by December whether the curve is symmetrical or not.

Mood inertia is a compelling concept. If you're right about the asymmetry, it could also suggest that the autumn transition is where targeted interventions — the lamp, increased exercise, social planning — would have the most leverage. You'd be optimizing for extending the buffer rather than trying to replace the daylight entirely. This is going to be a genuinely interesting dataset by the end of the year.

It is. And in the meantime, I am going to enjoy being at a 6.8 while it lasts. That is the highest sustained average I have recorded. Statistically, I am the happiest I have been in 6 months. Which is a very Swedish way to describe happiness — with a decimal point.
A happiness with a decimal point — I think that is perfectly valid. Enjoy the 6.8 and the 16 hours of light. Stockholm in spring has earned it.
